# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a test bench in Verilog. List any four characteristics that distinguish a test bench from a synthesizable RTL module.**

**Model Answer:**

A **test bench** is a non-synthesizable Verilog module that wraps around a Design Under Test (DUT) and provides stimulus inputs, observes outputs, and verifies functional correctness through simulation.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **No ports:** A test bench is a top-level module with no port declarations (`module tb_name;` — empty parenthesis).
2. **Uses `initial` blocks:** Test benches use `initial` blocks for stimulus generation, which have no hardware equivalent and are ignored by synthesis.
3. **Uses simulation-only constructs:** Statements like `$display`, `$monitor`, `$finish`, `$stop`, `$dumpfile`, and `#` delays are simulation-only and never synthesized.
4. **Contains `reg` and `wire` declarations internally:** All test bench signals are declared locally; they are not visible outside the test bench module.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the difference between `$monitor` and `$display` system tasks in Verilog with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `$display` | `$monitor` |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Executes **only once** when the line is reached | Executes **continuously** until simulation ends |
| Trigger | Triggered by procedural flow | Triggered whenever any argument **changes value** |
| Multiple calls | Calling `$display` again re-prints | Only the **last** `$monitor` call is active |

**Example:**

```verilog
// $display: prints once
initial begin
    $display("Value of A = %b", A);   // Prints only once at t=0
end

// $monitor: prints on every change
initial begin
    $monitor("A = %b, B = %b", A, B); // Prints whenever A or B changes
end
```

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting**
> The most common reasons students lose marks in test bench questions are:
> 1. **Forgetting `` `timescale `` directive** at the top — lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Declaring `wire` instead of `reg` for stimulus signals** that are driven by the test bench — causes compilation error, lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `<=`** in sequential stimulus (e.g., for a clock) — both work for `reg` in `initial`, but for `always` block, you must use `=` (blocking) for clock toggling.
> 4. **Forgetting to instantiate the DUT** — the test bench compiles but does nothing useful — lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not terminating simulation** with `$finish` — the simulator hangs indefinitely in some tools — lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not showing port connections by name** (`.A(A)`) — KTU prefers named association for clarity.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A 4-bit ripple carry adder is to be designed and verified.**

**(a)** Write the Verilog RTL code for a **1-bit full adder** (structural or behavioral) that will be used as a building block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Verilog **test bench** to verify the 1-bit full adder by applying all 8 input combinations and checking the outputs against expected values. The test bench should display a "TEST PASSED" or "TEST FAILED" message based on the comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 1-bit Full Adder RTL

```verilog
// Behavioral model of a 1-bit Full Adder
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module full_adder (
    input  A,
    input  B,
    input  C_in,
    output Sum,
    output C_out
);
    // Continuous assignment using boolean equations
    assign Sum   = A ^ B ^ C_in;
    assign C_out = (A & B) | (C_in & (A ^ B));
endmodule
```

**Marking Scheme Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Module declaration with port list and directions: 2 marks
- Correct Sum equation: 2 marks
- Correct Carry equation: 2 marks
- Proper `endmodule` and syntax: 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Self-Checking Test Bench

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module tb_full_adder;
    reg  A, B, C_in;
    wire Sum, C_out;
    reg  Sum_exp, C_out_exp;
    integer error_count;
    integer i;

    // DUT Instantiation with named port association
    full_adder uut (
        .A(A),
        .B(B),
        .C_in(C_in),
        .Sum(Sum),
        .C_out(C_out)
    );

    // Stimulus and Self-Checking Block
    initial begin
        error_count = 0;

        $display("Starting Full Adder Test Bench...");
        $display(" A B Cin | Sum Cout | Expected Sum Cout | Result");
        $display("----------------------------------------------");

        for (i = 0; i < 8; i = i + 1) begin
            {A, B, C_in} = i;          // Apply vector
            #10;                        // Wait for propagation

            // Compute expected outputs
            Sum_exp   = A ^ B ^ C_in;
            C_out_exp = (A & B) | (C_in & (A ^ B));

            // Compare
            if ((Sum === Sum_exp) && (C_out === C_out_exp))
                $display(" %b %b  %b  |  %b    %b   |    %b       %b     | PASS",
                          A, B, C_in, Sum, C_out, Sum_exp, C_out_exp);
            else begin
                $display(" %b %b  %b  |  %b    %b   |    %b       %b     | FAIL",
                          A, B, C_in, Sum, C_out, Sum_exp, C_out_exp);
                error_count = error_count + 1;
            end
        end

        // Final report
        if (error_count == 0)
            $display("\n*** TEST PASSED *** All 8 vectors verified.");
        else
            $display("\n*** TEST FAILED *** %0d errors found.", error_count);

        $finish;
    end
endmodule
```

**Marking Scheme Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct `reg`/`wire` declarations: 1 mark
- Correct DUT instantiation with named ports: 1 mark
- Loop iterating all 8 vectors: 1 mark
- Correct expected output computation: 1 mark
- Proper `if-else` comparison with `===` or `!==`: 1 mark
- Display of PASS/FAIL message and `$finish`: 1 mark
- Proper indentation, syntax, and `endmodule`: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A D flip-flop with asynchronous active-low reset and positive-edge clock is to be verified.**

**(a)** Write the Verilog behavioral model for the D flip-flop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Verilog test bench that generates a 10 ns period clock, applies asynchronous reset for 20 ns, and then applies 4 different data values (each held for 30 ns) to the D input. Use `$monitor` to display the values of `D`, `clk`, `rst`, and `Q` on the console. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — D Flip-Flop RTL

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module d_ff (
    input  D,
    input  clk,
    input  rst_n,        // Active-low asynchronous reset
    output reg Q
);
    // Asynchronous active-low reset, positive-edge clocked D flip-flop
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (rst_n == 1'b0)
            Q <= 1'b0;            // Reset overrides clock
        else
            Q <= D;               // Capture D on rising edge
    end
endmodule
```

**Marking Scheme Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Module declaration with `reg Q`: 1 mark
- Correct sensitivity list `posedge clk or negedge rst_n`: 2 marks
- Correct asynchronous reset branch: 2 marks
- Correct clocked data capture branch: 1 mark
- `endmodule`: 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — D Flip-Flop Test Bench

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module tb_d_ff;
    reg  D;
    reg  clk;
    reg  rst_n;
    wire Q;

    // DUT Instantiation
    d_ff uut (
        .D(D),
        .clk(clk),
        .rst_n(rst_n),
        .Q(Q)
    );

    // Clock Generator: 10 ns period (5 ns half-period)
    initial clk = 1'b0;
    always #5 clk = ~clk;

    // Stimulus and Reset Block
    initial begin
        // Initialize signals
        D     = 1'b0;
        rst_n = 1'b0;        // Assert active-low reset
        #20;                // Hold reset for 20 ns

        rst_n = 1'b1;        // Release reset
        #5;                 // Wait past next rising edge

        // Apply 4 different data values, each held for 30 ns
        D = 1'b1;  #30;
        D = 1'b0;  #30;
        D = 1'b1;  #30;
        D = 1'b0;  #30;

        $finish;
    end

    // Monitor Block: prints whenever any signal changes
    initial begin
        $monitor("Time=%0t ns | clk=%b rst_n=%b D=%b | Q=%b",
                  $time, clk, rst_n, D, Q);
    end

    // Waveform Dump
    initial begin
        $dumpfile("dff_wave.vcd");
        $dumpvars(0, tb_d_ff);
    end
endmodule
```

**Marking Scheme Breakdown (7 marks):**
- Correct `reg`/`wire` declarations: 1 mark
- Clock generator with `always #5 clk = ~clk;`: 1 mark
- Reset asserted for 20 ns then released: 1 mark
- Four data values applied with `#30` delays: 1 mark
- `$monitor` statement with format specifiers: 1 mark
- `$dumpfile` and `$dumpvars`: 1 mark
- `$finish` and proper `endmodule`: 1 mark

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **Definition:** A test bench is a **non-synthesizable**, **port-less** top-level Verilog module used to **simulate and verify** a Design Under Test (DUT).
- **Three pillars:** Stimulus generator → DUT instantiation → Response monitor.
- **`` `timescale `` directive** must appear at the **first line** of the file: `` `timescale 1ns/1ps ``.
- **Test bench signals:** Stimulus signals are declared `reg`; observed signals are declared `wire`.
- **DUT instantiation:** Use **named port association** (`.A(A)`) for clarity and safety.
- **Clock period formula:** For period $T$, use `always #(T/2) clk = ~clk;` — half-period delay.
- **`$display` vs `$monitor`:** `$display` is one-shot, `$monitor` is continuous.
- **`$stop` vs `$finish`:** `$stop` pauses, `$finish` terminates — use `$finish` at the end of automated test benches.
- **Self-checking pattern:** Use `!==` (non-case-equality) to catch `x` and `z` mismatches that `!=` would mask.
- **Exhaustive testing:** For $n$ inputs, apply all $2^n$ vectors — derived from $N = 2^n$.
- **File I/O:** Use `$readmemb` (binary) or `$readmemh` (hex) to load test vectors from an external file.
- **Waveform viewing:** Use `$dumpfile("name.vcd")` and `$dumpvars;` to generate VCD files for GTKWave/ModelSim.
- **Synthesis tool behavior:** The synthesizer **strips out** the test bench entirely — only the DUT is mapped to gates.
- **Task-based stimulus:** Encapsulate reusable stimulus sequences in `task` blocks for cleaner code.
- **Error counter pattern:** Maintain an `integer error_count` that increments on every mismatch — final pass/fail decision is based on `error_count == 0`.
- **Loop construct for vectors:** Use `for (i = 0; i < 2**n; i = i + 1)` and apply the bit-slice `{A, B, C_in} = i;` to drive inputs efficiently.
- **Initial block starts at t=0:** All `initial` blocks begin execution **simultaneously** at simulation time zero.
- **Random stimulus:** Use `$random` function for randomized testing; useful for coverage of corner cases.
- **Hierarchical referencing:** Use the instance name (e.g., `uut.Sum`) to access DUT signals from the test bench if needed.
- **Most common KTU exam question style:** "Write a Verilog test bench to verify the following design" with sub-parts (a) RTL and (b) test bench code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling a D Flip-Flop in Verilog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAEST305 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modeling a D Flip-Flop in Verilog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D Flip-Flop (Data Flip-Flop):** A **1-bit memory element** that samples the value present on its data input **D** at the active edge of a clock signal and holds that value on the output **Q** until the next active clock edge. It is the fundamental **synchronous sequential** building block of any digital system.

In the context of **Verilog HDL (Hardware Description Language)** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4 (Verilog Modeling of Sequential Circuits), a D Flip-Flop is described textually using one of three abstraction levels:

1. **Structural (Gate-Level) Modeling** – using primitive gates (`and`, `or`, `not`, `nand`).
2. **Dataflow Modeling** – using continuous assignments (`assign`) and Boolean expressions.
3. **Behavioral Modeling** – using `always` procedural blocks sensitive to clock edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The Board typically tests the **behavioral model** of a D Flip-Flop using the `always @(posedge clk)` construct, along with asynchronous/synchronous reset, since this maps directly to how real **FPGA** and **ASIC** flip-flops are inferred during synthesis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase Light Switch"

Imagine a staircase light that can be toggled **only at the instant** you press a master "capture" button (the clock edge):

- The **D input** is the position of the toggle switch (UP = 1, DOWN = 0) just before you press capture.
- The **clock edge** is your finger pressing the capture button — at that *single instant*, the light **locks in** the value of the switch.
- The **Q output** is the actual state of the light. It will *ignore* whatever you do to the toggle switch **between** capture presses.
- The light only updates again when you press the capture button the next time.

This perfectly captures the **edge-triggered, sample-and-hold** nature of a D Flip-Flop.

## 1.3 Why Model a D Flip-Flop in Verilog?

Modeling in Verilog allows the designer to:

- **Simulate** the circuit *before* fabricating silicon.
- **Synthesize** the model into real hardware gates (using tools like Xilinx Vivado, Synopsys Design Compiler, or Intel Quartus).
- **Reuse** the same description across FPGA prototypes and ASIC tape-outs.

> [!TIP]
> The D Flip-Flop is to sequential design what the **transistor** is to analog design — every register, counter, shift register, and state machine in a modern CPU is built from millions of these 1-bit memory cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the D Flip-Flop

The D Flip-Flop eliminates the *invalid state* problem of the JK Flip-Flop by ensuring the input is **always valid**. The characteristic equation is:

$$Q_{next} = D$$

That is, whatever is on the **D** line at the active clock edge becomes the next state of **Q**. There is no toggle, no race-around, and no forbidden input combination.

### 2.1.1 Key Behavioral Properties

- **Edge-Triggered:** State change occurs ONLY on the **0→1 (rising)** or **1→0 (falling)** transition of `clk`, NOT on the level.
- **Transparent Interval = 0:** Unlike a D-latch, the output is *never* transparent to the input.
- **Setup Time (t_su):** The time D must be stable *before* the active clock edge. KTU expects you to acknowledge this in exam answers.
- **Hold Time (t_h):** The time D must remain stable *after* the active clock edge.

## 2.2 The Three Verilog Modeling Styles — Compared

| Modeling Style | Verilog Construct | Abstraction | KTU Relevance | Synthesis Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | `nand`, `nor`, `and` gate primitives | Gate-level (lowest) | Asked in 3-mark Qs | Mapped to standard cell library |
| **Dataflow** | `assign Q = D;` | RTL (medium) | Rarely used alone for FF | Inferred as a **buffer/wire** (no memory!) |
| **Behavioral** | `always @(posedge clk)` | RTL (highest) | **Most important for KTU** | Inferred as a real **D Flip-Flop** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing `assign Q = D;` and thinking you have a Flip-Flop. **A Flip-Flop CANNOT be modeled with a continuous assignment** — it has no memory. The continuous assignment will simply create a combinational wire, not a sequential element.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Characteristic Equation | $Q_{next} = D$ | Dimensionless, Boolean |
| 2 | Excitation Equation | $D = Q_{next}$ | Same as above (D-FF is self-excited) |
| 3 | Hold Condition (D-FF) | $D = Q_{next}$ | Always satisfied, no toggle |
| 4 | Maximum Clock Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{su} + t_{co} + t_{comb}}$ | Hz (Hertz) |
| 5 | Setup Time Constraint | $t_{clk \rightarrow Q} + t_{comb} \leq T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | Seconds |
| 6 | Verilog Sensitivity (Posedge) | `always @(posedge clk)` | Rising-edge triggered |
| 7 | Verilog Sensitivity (Negedge) | `always @(negedge clk)` | Falling-edge triggered |
| 8 | Non-Blocking Assignment | `Q <= D;` | **Mandatory** for FFs in sequential logic |
| 9 | Blocking Assignment | `Q = D;` | Forbidden in FF modeling — causes race conditions |
| 10 | Asynchronous Reset | `always @(posedge clk or posedge rst)` | Independent of clock |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "**<=**" vs "**=**" rule** is the single most-tested Verilog concept in KTU Module 4. Non-blocking assignments in `always @(posedge clk)` blocks model real flip-flop behavior with parallel hardware update semantics.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

D Flip-Flops modeled in Verilog are the **synthesis primitive** for:

- **Pipeline registers** in CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen use billions of them).
- **Shift registers** in serializers/deserializers (SerDes) for USB, PCIe, Ethernet.
- **State registers** in Finite State Machines (FSMs) controlling traffic lights, elevators, and washing machines.
- **Memory elements** in Static RAM (SRAM) cell arrays.
- **Synchronizers** for crossing clock domains in System-on-Chip (SoC) designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Characteristic Equation

We start from the **excitation table** of the D Flip-Flop, which describes the required D input to produce a given state transition:

| Current State $Q$ | Next State $Q_{next}$ | Required Input $D$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 |

### Step 1 — Build the Truth Table

From the table above, we observe that **D must always equal $Q_{next}$**. This is because a D Flip-Flop has no toggle or hold modes — the data line is *directly* transferred to the output on the clock edge.

### Step 2 — Express as a Boolean Equation

$$D = Q_{next}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Characteristic Equation

Since the next state of the Flip-Flop is *always* the value of D at the active clock edge:

$$Q_{next} = D$$

### Step 4 — Verification using a Karnaugh Map

A K-map with $Q$ (rows) and $D$ (columns) yields two group-of-one cells at positions $(Q=0, D=1)$ and $(Q=1, D=1)$. The minimal sum-of-products is:

$$Q_{next} = D$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The K-map confirms there is **no dependency on Q** itself — the next state is purely a function of the D input. This is what makes the D Flip-Flop the **simplest** 1-bit memory element in digital design.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Verilog Modeling Styles of D Flip-Flop

### 3.2.1 Model 1 — Behavioral Model (KTU's Most-Frequently-Asked)

```verilog
// File: dff_behavioral.v
// Module: D Flip-Flop - Behavioral Modeling Style
// Course: GAEST305 - Module 4 - KTU 2024 Scheme

module dff_behavioral (
    input  wire clk,   // Clock input (active on rising edge)
    input  wire D,     // Data input
    output reg  Q,     // Output (declared as 'reg' since assigned in always block)
    output wire Qbar   // Complementary output
);

    // Sensitivity list contains only the active clock edge
    // This infers a POSITIVE-EDGE-TRIGGERED D Flip-Flop
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        Q <= D;        // Non-blocking assignment: models real hardware
    end

    // Qbar is combinational - continuously the inverse of Q
    assign Qbar = ~Q;

endmodule
```

**Line-by-line explanation for KTU answers:**

- `input wire clk, D` — declare inputs as wires (default for ports).
- `output reg Q` — Q must be `reg` because it is assigned inside an `always` block.
- `output wire Qbar` — Qbar is assigned via `assign`, so it remains a `wire`.
- `always @(posedge clk)` — sensitivity list contains ONLY the posedge event of `clk`, making it a **rising-edge-triggered** Flip-Flop.
- `Q <= D;` — non-blocking assignment. The RHS is evaluated first, then ALL RHS values are assigned to LHS simultaneously at the end of the time step. This models the **parallel hardware** behavior of real Flip-Flops.

### 3.2.2 Model 2 — Behavioral Model with Asynchronous Reset

```verilog
module dff_async_reset (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst,    // Active-high asynchronous reset
    input  wire D,
    output reg  Q
);

    // Asynchronous reset: rst is in the sensitivity list with posedge
    // This means reset takes effect IMMEDIATELY, regardless of clk
    always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin
        if (rst)
            Q <= 1'b0;  // Force Q to 0 the moment rst goes high
        else
            Q <= D;     // Normal operation on clock edge
    end

endmodule
```

> [!TIP]
> The word **"or posedge rst"** in the sensitivity list is what makes the reset **asynchronous**. If you write `always @(posedge clk)` and put the reset check inside an `if` block, the reset becomes **synchronous** — a classic KTU question.

### 3.2.3 Model 3 — Behavioral Model with Synchronous Reset

```verilog
module dff_sync_reset (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst,    // Active-high synchronous reset
    input  wire D,
    output reg  Q
);

    // Only clk is in the sensitivity list
    // Reset is checked only on the active clock edge
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (rst)
            Q <= 1'b0;
        else
            Q <= D;
    end

endmodule
```

### 3.2.4 Model 4 — Structural (Gate-Level) Model Using NAND Gates

The classic D Flip-Flop is built from **4 NAND gates** plus an **inverter** (for the D input) forming a master-slave SR latch, with the D input removing the invalid state.

```verilog
module dff_structural (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire D,
    output wire Q,
    output wire Qbar
);

    // Step 1: Inverter to create Dbar from D
    not  (Dbar, D);

    // Step 2: Master SR Latch (NAND-based)
    // S1 and R1 are set/reset for the master, active when clk = 0
    nand (S1, D,   ~clk);   // Set input to master, gated by NOT clk
    nand (R1, Dbar, ~clk);  // Reset input to master

    // Master latch outputs
    nand (Qm,  S1, Qmbar);
    nand (Qmbar, R1, Qm);

    // Step 3: Slave SR Latch, gated by clk
    nand (S2, Qm,    clk);
    nand (R2, Qmbar, clk);

    // Final outputs
    nand (Q,    S2, Qbar);
    nand (Qbar, R2, Q);

endmodule
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU may ask you to draw a NAND-based D Flip-Flop. Remember: the **master** is active when `clk = 0`, the **slave** is active when `clk = 1`. This two-stage arrangement is what gives the D Flip-Flop its **edge-triggered** behavior.

## 3.3 Testbench (KTU Often Asks You to Write One)

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps

module tb_dff;
    reg  clk, D;
    wire Q, Qbar;

    // Instantiate the D Flip-Flop Under Test (DUT)
    dff_behavioral uut (.clk(clk), .D(D), .Q(Q), .Qbar(Qbar));

    // Clock generation: 10 ns period (100 MHz)
    initial clk = 0;
    always #5 clk = ~clk;

    // Stimulus
    initial begin
        D = 0;   #12;   // D = 0 for 12 ns
        D = 1;   #10;   // D = 1 for 10 ns
        D = 0;   #8;    // D = 0 for 8 ns
        D = 1;   #15;   // D = 1 for 15 ns
        $finish;
    end
endmodule
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 D Flip-Flop — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input D] -- data path --> B[MUX controlled by reset signal]
    C[Asynchronous reset rst] -- control --> B
    D[Clock signal clk] -- posedge trigger --> E[Edge detector and storage element]
    B -- muxed data --> E
    E -- Q output --> F[Output Q]
    E -- inverted Q --> G[Output Qbar]
    H[Setup time monitor] -.-> E
    I[Hold time monitor] -.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of a D Flip-Flop with asynchronous reset. The data path is selected by a MUX whose select line is the reset signal, and the storage is triggered on the `posedge` of the clock. The `.-` dotted links indicate timing-constraint monitors (setup/hold) that are not physical hardware but are checked by the synthesis tool.

## 4.2 Verilog Synthesis Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Verilog Source Code with always posedge clk] --> B[Lexical and Syntax Analysis]
    B --> C[Elaboration and Hierarchy Resolution]
    C --> D[Synthesis Tool Infers D Flip-Flop Primitive]
    D --> E[Technology Mapping to FPGA or ASIC Cell]
    E --> F[Place and Route on Silicon or FPGA Fabric]
    F --> G[Physical D Flip-Flop Hardware]
    H[Non-blocking assignment Q le D] -.-> A
    I[Blocking assignment Q eq D] -.-> X[Synthesis Warning or Latch Inference]
    X --> Y[Incorrect Sequential Element]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This flow illustrates why **non-blocking assignment (`<=`)** is non-negotiable in FF modeling. The `Q = D;` (blocking) path leads to incorrect latch inference or simulation/synthesis mismatch, costing the student marks and the engineer a chip re-spin.

## 4.3 Waveform Behavior Diagram (Behavioral Intuition)

```mermaid
gantt
    title D Flip-Flop Timing Diagram (Conceptual)
    dateFormat ss.SSS
    axisFormat %S.%L
    section Clock
    CLK_LOW_1      :a1, 00.000, 500ms
    CLK_HIGH_1     :a2, after a1, 500ms
    section Data Input
    D_STABLE_LOW   :b1, 00.000, 1500ms
    D_GOES_HIGH    :b2, after b1, 500ms
    section Q Output
    Q_PREVIOUS     :c1, 00.000, 1000ms
    Q_CAPTURE_HIGH :c2, after c1, 1000ms
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between blocking and non-blocking procedural assignments in Verilog. Why are non-blocking assignments used in D Flip-Flop modeling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Blocking (`=`) | Non-blocking (`<=`) |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Sequential, one statement completes before the next | RHS evaluated first, ALL assignments happen at end of time step |
| Hardware model | Sequential execution (like C code) | Parallel hardware update (like real Flip-Flops) |
| Use case | Combinational `always @(*)` blocks | Sequential `always @(posedge clk)` blocks |

**D Flip-Flop use:** In `always @(posedge clk)`, all Flip-Flops must update **simultaneously** on the same clock edge. The non-blocking assignment `<=` ensures that the RHS of every FF is sampled *first* (using the *old* Q values) and only then are all LHS values updated. This prevents **race conditions** and accurately models parallel hardware. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the characteristic equation of a D Flip-Flop? Justify why a D Flip-Flop has no invalid state, unlike a JK Flip-Flop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$Q_{next} = D$$

**Justification:** In a JK Flip-Flop, the J=K=1 condition causes a *toggle*, but if the clock pulse is wider than the propagation delay, the output can toggle multiple times — this is the *race-around condition*. The D Flip-Flop has only **one** data input, and the output is *forced* to follow D at the active clock edge. Since D can only be 0 or 1, there is no ambiguous input combination, and hence no invalid state. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]`

**a)** Write the Verilog behavioral model of a **positive-edge-triggered D Flip-Flop with asynchronous active-high reset and an active-low enable input**. Use a separate testbench to verify the functionality. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of the above D Flip-Flop for the input sequence: `D = 1, 0, 1, 1, 0` with `enable = 1, 0, 1, 1, 1` and `rst = 0, 0, 0, 1, 0` applied at successive rising clock edges. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Verilog Code (7 marks):**

```verilog
module dff_async_rst_en (
    input  wire clk,      // Clock input
    input  wire rst,      // Asynchronous active-high reset
    input  wire en,       // Active-low enable (per the question)
    input  wire D,        // Data input
    output reg  Q         // Output
);

    // Asynchronous reset: rst is in sensitivity list
    always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin
        if (rst)
            Q <= 1'b0;             // Asynchronous reset to 0
        else if (!en)              // en is active-low, so !en means enable is ON
            Q <= D;                // Capture D on clock edge
        else
            Q <= Q;                // Hold previous value (no change)
    end
endmodule
```

**Testbench (additional 2 marks within the 7):**

```verilog
`timescale 1ns/1ps
module tb_dff_async_rst_en;
    reg  clk, rst, en, D;
    wire Q;

    dff_async_rst_en uut (.clk(clk), .rst(rst), .en(en), .D(D), .Q(Q));

    initial clk = 0;
    always #5 clk = ~clk;          // 10 ns clock period

    initial begin
        rst = 1; en = 0; D = 0;    // Apply reset
        #12 rst = 0;               // Release reset
        #10 D = 1;                 // D = 1
        #10 en = 1; D = 0;         // Disable, D = 0
        #10 en = 0; D = 1;         // Enable, D = 1
        #10 rst = 1;               // Apply async reset
        #10 rst = 0; en = 0; D = 0;
        #20 $finish;
    end
endmodule
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Module declaration and port list: **1 mark**
- Sensitivity list with `posedge clk or posedge rst`: **2 marks**
- Correct use of non-blocking assignment `<=`: **1 mark**
- Correct logic for reset/enable/hold: **2 marks**
- Testbench with clock and stimulus: **1 mark**

---

**(b) Timing Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

Let's trace the behavior at each rising clock edge (assuming clock period = 10 ns, starting at t = 5 ns):

| Clock Edge | rst | en (active-low) | D | Action | Q (new) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (t=5) | 0 | 1 (disabled) | 1 | en is high → HOLD | Q stays at 0 |
| 2 (t=15) | 0 | 0 (enabled) | 0 | en is low → CAPTURE D | Q = 0 |
| 3 (t=25) | 0 | 1 (disabled) | 1 | HOLD | Q = 0 |
| 4 (t=35) | 1 | 0 (enabled) | 1 | Async reset overrides | Q = 0 |
| 5 (t=45) | 0 | 1 (disabled) | 0 | HOLD | Q = 0 |

**Key points for full marks (7):**
- Recognize asynchronous reset acts *independently* of clock (1 mark)
- Recognize that the active-low enable must be `0` for D to be captured (1 mark)
- Correctly tabulate state transitions (3 marks)
- Verbal explanation of race-condition avoidance using `<=` (2 marks)

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**a)** Implement a **negative-edge-triggered D Flip-Flop** using **structural (gate-level) Verilog modeling** with NAND gates. Draw the internal schematic. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare **synchronous reset** versus **asynchronous reset** in D Flip-Flop design. Under what condition is each preferred in industry practice? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Structural Implementation (7 marks):**

```verilog
module dff_negedge_structural (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire D,
    output wire Q,
    output wire Qbar
);
    wire Dbar, clkbar, S1, R1, S2, R2;

    // Input inverter
    not (Dbar, D);

    // Invert clock for negedge triggering
    not (clkbar, clk);

    // Master latch (active when clk = 1, i.e., clkbar = 0)
    nand (S1, D,    clkbar);
    nand (R1, Dbar, clkbar);
    nand (Qm,    S1, Qmbar);
    nand (Qmbar, R1, Qm);

    // Slave latch (active when clk = 0, i.e., clkbar = 1)
    nand (S2, Qm,    clk);
    nand (R2, Qmbar, clk);
    nand (Q,    S2, Qbar);
    nand (Qbar, R2, Q);

endmodule
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct inverter for D and clock: **1 mark**
- Master latch with NAND gates: **2 marks**
- Slave latch with NAND gates: **2 marks**
- Proper interconnection (Qm → slave, Qmbar → slave): **2 marks**

---

**(b) Synchronous vs Asynchronous Reset (7 marks):**

| Property | Synchronous Reset | Asynchronous Reset |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity list | `always @(posedge clk)` only | `always @(posedge clk or posedge rst)` |
| Activation timing | Only on active clock edge | Immediately, regardless of clock |
| Reset recovery/removal | Not required | **Required** (must deassert rst before next clock edge) |
| Glitch sensitivity | Immune to glitches on rst | Sensitive to glitches on rst |
| FPGA fabric usage | Uses FFs with synchronous reset pin | Uses dedicated global reset network (SR) |
| Power consumption | Lower (no extra routing) | Higher (global reset tree) |
| Industry preference | ASIC designs | FPGA designs (Xilinx, Intel) |

**When to use:**
- **Asynchronous reset** is preferred in **FPGA-based** designs because FPGAs have dedicated, low-skew global reset networks.
- **Synchronous reset** is preferred in **ASIC** designs because it guarantees clean reset release synchronized to the clock domain, avoiding metastability.
- **Modern practice:** Many designs use a **synchronous reset** internally for clean deassertion, but assert it asynchronously using a **reset synchronizer** circuit. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
> 1. **Using `assign Q = D;`** — This is NOT a Flip-Flop. The examiner will give **0 marks** for memory behavior.
> 2. **Using blocking `=` instead of non-blocking `<=`** — Causes race conditions. Deduct **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting `posedge`/`negedge`** in the sensitivity list — Makes it a latch, not a Flip-Flop. Deduct **2 marks**.
> 4. **Declaring `Q` as `wire` instead of `reg`** when using `always` — **Compilation error** in Verilog. Deduct **1 mark** for syntax.
> 5. **Confusing synchronous vs asynchronous reset** — The presence/absence of the reset signal in the sensitivity list is what defines them. Examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Characteristic equation:** $Q_{next} = D$ — the simplest of all Flip-Flops.
- **Three Verilog modeling styles:** Structural (gates), Dataflow (`assign`), Behavioral (`always @(posedge clk)`).
- **Behavioral is king** for KTU exams on this topic.
- **Non-blocking assignment `<=`** is **mandatory** in `always @(posedge clk)` blocks.
- **Q must be declared as `reg`**, not `wire`, when assigned inside `always`.
- **Asynchronous reset:** Reset signal is in the sensitivity list (`or posedge rst`).
- **Synchronous reset:** Reset signal is checked *inside* the `always` block, not in the sensitivity list.
- **Sensitivity list rule:** For posedge-triggered FF → `always @(posedge clk)`; for negedge → `always @(negedge clk)`.
- **Hold value:** `Q <= Q;` (or omit the else branch) keeps the previous state.
- **Setup time (t_su)** and **hold time (t_h)** are physical timing constraints checked by synthesis tools.
- **Master-slave NAND structure** gives edge-triggered behavior; the master is transparent when `clk=0`, the slave when `clk=1` (for posedge DFF).
- **Industry usage:** D Flip-Flops are the backbone of all pipeline registers, state machines, shift registers, and memory elements in CPUs, GPUs, and SoCs.
- **Common KTU traps:** Using `assign` for FFs, using `=` instead of `<=`, forgetting `posedge/negedge`, mismatched `wire`/`reg` declarations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling an FSM in Verilog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAEST305 - DIGITAL ELECTRONICS AND LOGIC DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modeling an FSM in Verilog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Modeling an FSM in Verilog

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Finite State Machine (FSM)** is a sequential digital system whose behaviour is mathematically modelled as a finite set of **states**, **inputs**, **outputs**, and a pair of transition functions that govern the next state and the output generation. When an FSM is *modelled in Verilog*, the designer translates this abstract mathematical machine into a synthesizable **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** representation consisting of a *state register* (typically implemented as D-flip-flops) and *combinational next-state* and *output* logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GAEST305, Module 4):**
> *"A Finite State Machine (FSM) is a sequential circuit whose output depends not only on the present input but also on the present state. In Verilog, an FSM is modelled using two (or three) `always` blocks: one sequential block for state memory and one (or two) combinational blocks for next-state and output logic."*

In Verilog, an FSM is described using one of two canonical machine models:

1. **Moore Machine** – The output is a function of the *present state only*.
2. **Mealy Machine** – The output is a function of the *present state **and** the current input*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every digital design problem (sequence detectors, traffic-light controllers, vending machines, UART receivers) eventually reduces to an FSM description. KTU examiners test whether you can convert a state diagram into valid, synthesizable Verilog code using the **three-block coding style** (recommended for KTU board answers).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **lift (elevator) in a 3-storey building**:

- The lift can only be in one of a *finite* number of positions (Ground, Floor 1, Floor 2). These are its **states**.
- When you press a button (input), the lift decides which state to transition to.
- The display showing the current floor (output) is purely a function of *where the lift currently is* — this is exactly the **Moore** behaviour.
- If the lift door were to open/close *depending on the button being pressed AND the current floor*, that would be a **Mealy** behaviour.

**In Verilog terms:** The lift's internal memory (its current floor) is captured in a `reg [1:0] state;` register, while the combinational decision logic (which floor to go to next) is captured inside a `case (state)` block inside a combinational `always` block.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning | Physical Realisation in Verilog |
|---|---|---|
| **State** | Memory element of the FSM | A `reg` updated inside a sequential `always @(posedge clk)` block |
| **Present State (PS)** | Current memory value | Output of the state register |
| **Next State (NS)** | Computed value for the next clock | Computed by combinational logic and loaded into the register |
| **State Transition** | PS → NS based on input | Encoded as `case` / `if-else` |
| **Output Logic** | Moore: f(PS); Mealy: f(PS, input) | A separate `always` block or `assign` statement |
| **Reset** | Asynchronous/Synchronous initialisation | Triggered by an `rst` signal in the sequential block |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU metric:** A 2-bit register can represent up to **2ⁿ = 4 states**, an *n*-bit register up to **2ⁿ** states. This dictates the **state encoding width** that examiners expect you to declare in the port list.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an FSM in Verilog

A textbook-KTU FSM Verilog module is decomposed into **three logical sections**:

### Section A — State Encoding & Parameters

The state space is declared using `localparam` (preferred over `parameter` for KTU board answers, as it is non-overridable inside the module).

$$
\text{Number of bits required to encode } N \text{ states} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Three standard encoding styles**:
> - **Binary / Sequential** — Minimum number of bits; requires fewer flip-flops but may cause multiple bit glitches during transition.
> - **One-Hot** — One flip-flop per state; *N* states ⇒ *N* flip-flops. Faster decoding, easier to debug, preferred for **FPGA** synthesis.
> - **Gray** — Only one bit changes between adjacent states; minimum glitches, used in counters and clock-domain crossings.

### Section B — State Register (Sequential `always` block)

This block models the **state memory** and is sensitive to either `posedge clk` or `negedge clk`. The reset may be **asynchronous** (recommended in KTU textbooks) or **synchronous**.

### Section C — Next-State & Output Logic (Combinational `always` block)

This block uses a `case (state)` statement to compute the next state. Inside the same `case` block, you may also drive the Moore outputs. For Mealy outputs, a separate `always` block or an `assign` driven by both `state` and `input` is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Default Case" Rule:** Every `case` statement in KTU board answers must end with a `default:` clause that assigns a known safe value (e.g., `next_state = S0;`). Failing to do so will cause the synthesis tool to infer an unintended latch — and KTU examiners deduct marks for "possible inferred latch".

---

## 2.2 Moore vs. Mealy — Comparative Analysis

| Property | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Output dependence** | State only | State **+** Input |
| **Number of states (for same task)** | Usually **more** | Usually **fewer** |
| **Output timing** | Synchronous (one-cycle delay guaranteed) | Asynchronous (changes with input) |
| **Glitch sensitivity** | Low | High (output may glitch with input) |
| **Verilog structure** | 2 `always` blocks (state + next/output) | 3 `always` blocks (state + next + output) OR output as `assign` |
| **Typical KTU use-case** | Traffic-light controller, simple counters | Sequence detectors (1011), vending machines |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Number of states to flip-flops | $\text{FFs} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Determines `reg [N-1:0] state;` width |
| One-hot encoding FFs | $\text{FFs} = N$ | For FPGA implementation, one-hot is fastest |
| State transitions (Mealy) | $\delta : S \times I \rightarrow S$ | Maps to `case (state)` nested in `case (input)` |
| Output (Moore) | $\lambda : S \rightarrow O$ | Drives output *only* from `state` |
| Output (Mealy) | $\lambda : S \times I \rightarrow O$ | Output = `f(state, input)` |
| Reset recovery time | $t_{\text{rec}} \geq t_{\text{setup}} + t_{\text{ck-q}}$ | Critical for asynchronous resets in ASIC |
| Maximum clock frequency | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{\text{ck-q}} + t_{\text{comb}} + t_{\text{setup}}}$ | Bounds FSM throughput |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board examinations, when asked to "design an FSM in Verilog", you **must** include the explicit `default:` case, declare state codes using `localparam`, and clearly comment the *Moore/Mealy classification* at the top of the module. These three points alone account for **3–4 of the 14 marks** in a typical Part-B question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — 1011 Sequence Detector (Mealy, Overlapping)

This is the **most-asked FSM problem** in KTU 2024 scheme examinations. We will derive the full Verilog model step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Identify the State Space

We need to remember how many `1`s and `0`s we have seen in the correct sequence so far.

$$
S_0: \text{Initial / no useful prefix}
$$
$$
S_1: \text{Seen "1"}
$$
$$
S_2: \text{Seen "10"}
$$
$$
S_3: \text{Seen "101"}
$$

A 4-state machine, so the register width is $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ bits.

### Step 2 — State Transition Table (Mealy)

| Present State | Input `din` = 0 | Input `din` = 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **S0** | S0, out=0 | S1, out=0 |
| **S1** | S2, out=0 | S1, out=0 |
| **S2** | S0, out=0 | S3, out=0 |
| **S3** | S2, out=0 | S1, out=1 ← Detection! |

### Step 3 — Derive the Verilog Code

```verilog
// Mealy non-overlapping 1011 sequence detector
// KTU 2024 scheme reference model
module seq_detect_1011_mealy (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst_n,   // active-low asynchronous reset
    input  wire din,
    output reg  dout
);

    // State encoding using localparam
    localparam S0 = 2'b00,
               S1 = 2'b01,
               S2 = 2'b10,
               S3 = 2'b11;

    reg [1:0] state, next_state;

    // ---------- Block 1: State Register (Sequential) ----------
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            state <= S0;
        else
            state <= next_state;
    end

    // ---------- Block 2: Next-State Logic (Combinational) ----------
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: next_state = (din) ? S1 : S0;
            S1: next_state = (din) ? S1 : S2;
            S2: next_state = (din) ? S3 : S0;
            S3: next_state = (din) ? S1 : S2;
            default: next_state = S0;
        endcase
    end

    // ---------- Block 3: Mealy Output Logic ----------
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: dout = 1'b0;
            S1: dout = 1'b0;
            S2: dout = 1'b0;
            S3: dout = (din) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
            default: dout = 1'b0;
        endcase
    end

endmodule
```

### Step 4 — Analytical Justification (each line traced)

1. **`localparam S0 ... S3`** — Explicit symbolic names; readable and synthesis-friendly.
2. **`state <= next_state`** inside `posedge clk` — Models the D-flip-flop register.
3. **`always @(*)`** — Verilog-2001 sensitivity list shorthand; any change in `state` or `din` re-evaluates the block.
4. **Nested ternary `(din) ? S1 : S0`** — Implements the transition logic for column of the state table.
5. **`dout = (din) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0` inside S3** — This is the **Mealy output**: it is a function of *both* `state` and `din`, hence it changes combinationally with `din`, not synchronously with `clk`.

> [!NOTE]
> If the question demands a **Moore** version, we would add an extra state **S4** ("1011 detected") and assert `dout = 1` only when `state == S4`. The Moore version requires 5 states ⇒ $\lceil \log_2 5 \rceil = 3$ flip-flops.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Symptom in Code | KTU Mark Deduction |
|---|---|---|
| Missing `default:` in `case` | Synthesis infers a latch | **−2 marks** |
| Using `=` instead of `<=` in sequential block | Latch inferred / simulation mismatch | **−3 marks** |
| `always @(state)` (single signal) | Fails to update on `din` change | **−2 marks** |
| Forgetting asynchronous reset sensitivity | Reset works only when `clk` toggles | **−2 marks** |
| Output declared as `wire` but assigned in `always` | Compilation error | **−3 marks** |
| One-hot encoding declared as binary | Synthesis mismatch with simulation | **−2 marks** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Verilog FSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clk1[Clock clk] --> regBlock
    rst1[Async Reset rst_n] --> regBlock
    in1[Input din] --> combNext[Combinational Next-State Logic case state]
    in1 --> combOut[Combinational Output Logic Mealy or Moore]
    regBlock[Sequential State Register always posedge clk] --> combNext
    regBlock --> combOut
    combNext --> regBlock
    combOut --> out1[Output dout]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double-headed arrow between `regBlock` and `combNext` represents the feedback that closes the FSM loop. In Mealy machines, the `din` line also feeds `combOut` directly (combinational path).

## 4.2 State Diagram — Mealy 1011 Detector

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s0 : din=0 / out=0
    s0 --> s1 : din=1 / out=0
    s1 --> s1 : din=1 / out=0
    s1 --> s2 : din=0 / out=0
    s2 --> s0 : din=0 / out=0
    s2 --> s3 : din=1 / out=0
    s3 --> s2 : din=0 / out=0
    s3 --> s1 : din=1 / out=1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read the labels:** `s3 -- din=1 / out=1 --> s1` means *"When the machine is in state S3 and input is 1, the next state is S1 and the Mealy output is 1"*. This is the *detection* edge.

## 4.3 Three-Block Coding Style Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Module["module seq_detect_1011_mealy"]
        P["Port Declaration input clk, rst_n, din output reg dout"]
        E["localparam S0=00, S1=01, S2=10, S3=11"]
        R["reg [1:0] state, next_state"]
    end
    subgraph B1["Block 1 - State Register"]
        B1a["always posedge clk or negedge rst_n"]
        B1b["if reset then state lt= S0 else state lt= next_state"]
    end
    subgraph B2["Block 2 - Next State Logic"]
        B2a["always at star"]
        B2b["case state ... default next_state = S0 endcase"]
    end
    subgraph B3["Block 3 - Output Logic"]
        B3a["Mealy: always at star case state dout = f state, din endcase"]
        B3b["Moore: dout depends only on state"]
    end
    Module --> B1 --> B2 --> B3
```

## 4.4 Reset Topology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is reset asynchronous?} -->|Yes| A1[Use or negedge rst_n in sensitivity list]
    Q1 -->|No| S1[Use synchronous reset inside if posedge clk]
    A1 --> A2[FPGA / ASIC global reset networks]
    S1 --> S2[Cleaner timing closure; used in some Xilinx cores]
    A2 --> Final[Recommended for KTU 2024 board answers]
    S2 --> Final
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between a Moore machine and a Mealy machine. State one Verilog construct that distinguishes their implementation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Moore | Mealy |
|---|---|---|
| Output function | f(state) | f(state, input) |
| Output timing | Synchronous with clock | Asynchronous w.r.t. input |
| Verilog block | Output logic inside same `case (state)` block referencing *only* `state` | Separate `assign` or `always` block that references *both* `state` and `input` |

*Verilog distinguishing construct:* The presence of an input signal (e.g., `din`) on the **right-hand side** of the output assignment indicates a Mealy machine; absence indicates Moore. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for timing, 1 mark for Verilog distinction.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Why is the `default:` case mandatory inside the next-state `case` statement of an FSM? What hardware does its absence infer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The `default:` case guarantees that for any *unreachable* or *X-propagation* state value, the FSM deterministically transitions to a *known safe state* (typically `S0`).
2. Its absence causes the synthesis tool to **infer a level-sensitive latch** to hold the previous value of `next_state`, which is asynchronous and introduces glitches in the synthesised netlist.
3. Latches are generally undesirable in synchronous design and are flagged as a **design warning** by tools like Synopsys Design Compiler and Vivado.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) for Module 4 typically allows an **internal choice** between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sequence Detector Design

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the state diagram of a **Mealy machine** that detects the overlapping sequence **"1101"** from a serial input line `x`. Label all states, transitions, and outputs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete, synthesizable **Verilog module** for the above FSM using the three-block coding style with **asynchronous active-low reset** and **binary state encoding**. Include a `default:` case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — State Diagram**

States: $S_0$ (idle), $S_1$ (seen `1`), $S_2$ (seen `11`), $S_3$ (seen `110`).

$$
\text{Detection occurs on transition } S_3 \xrightarrow{x=1 \;/\; z=1} S_1
$$

| Present State | x = 0 | x = 1 |
|---|---|---|
| S0 | S0 / z=0 | S1 / z=0 |
| S1 | S0 / z=0 | S2 / z=0 |
| S2 | S3 / z=0 | S2 / z=0 |
| S3 | S0 / z=0 | S1 / z=**1** |

State transition explanation:
- From $S_3$, if the next bit is `1`, we have just consumed the *fourth* bit of "1101" — so we *output 1* and stay in $S_1$ because the last `1` can be the start of the next overlapping "1101".
- From $S_3$, if the next bit is `0`, the pattern is broken; we return to $S_0$.

**Part (b) — Verilog Code**

```verilog
module seq_1101_mealy (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst_n,
    input  wire x,
    output reg  z
);
    localparam S0 = 2'b00,
               S1 = 2'b01,
               S2 = 2'b10,
               S3 = 2'b11;

    reg [1:0] state, next_state;

    // Block 1: State register
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            state <= S0;
        else
            state <= next_state;
    end

    // Block 2: Next-state logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: next_state = (x) ? S1 : S0;
            S1: next_state = (x) ? S2 : S0;
            S2: next_state = (x) ? S2 : S3;
            S3: next_state = (x) ? S1 : S0;
            default: next_state = S0;
        endcase
    end

    // Block 3: Mealy output
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0: z = 1'b0;
            S1: z = 1'b0;
            S2: z = 1'b0;
            S3: z = (x) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;
            default: z = 1'b0;
        endcase
    end
endmodule
```

**Mark Distribution (Board Pattern):**
- Correct state diagram and transition table — **3 marks**
- Localparam encoding and port list — **1 mark**
- Correct sequential block with async reset — **2 marks**
- Correct next-state logic — **3 marks**
- Correct Mealy output block — **2 marks**
- `default:` case and comments — **1 mark**
- Compile-clean syntax — **2 marks** *(often assessed in lab, but counted in ESE for design questions)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Moore Machine Counter Design

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Design a **Moore FSM** that cycles through the states **S0 → S1 → S2 → S3 → S0** indefinitely, producing a single output `y` that is `1` **only** in state `S3`. Derive the state transition table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **complete Verilog code** for the Moore FSM using one-hot encoding. Verify that exactly one state bit is high at any time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — State Table (Moore)**

For 4 states, $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ FFs are required.

| Present State | Next State | Output y |
|---|---|---|
| S0 | S1 | 0 |
| S1 | S2 | 0 |
| S2 | S3 | 0 |
| S3 | S0 | 1 |

Moore property: **Output depends *only* on the present state** — so `y` is *unconditionally* 1 in $S_3$, regardless of any input.

**Part (b) — Verilog (One-Hot Encoding)**

```verilog
module moore_cycle_onehot (
    input  wire clk,
    input  wire rst_n,
    output reg  y
);
    // One-hot encoding: one bit per state
    localparam S0 = 4'b0001,
               S1 = 4'b0010,
               S2 = 4'b0100,
               S3 = 4'b1000;

    reg [3:0] state, next_state;

    // Block 1: State register
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n)
            state <= S0;
        else
            state <= next_state;
    end

    // Block 2: Next-state logic
    always @(*) begin
        case (state)
            S0:    next_state = S1;
            S1:    next_state = S2;
            S2:    next_state = S3;
            S3:    next_state = S0;
            default: next_state = S0;
        endcase
    end

    // Block 3: Moore output — y = state[S3]
    always @(*) begin
        y = state[3];   // 1 only when state == S3 (one-hot)
    end
endmodule
```

**Verification of one-hot property:** At any valid clock edge, exactly one bit of `state` is high — e.g., `state == 4'b0010` ⇒ only `S1` is active, `y = state[3] = 0`.

**Mark Distribution:**
- State transition table with Moore property justification — **3 marks**
- Verilog port and encoding declaration — **1 mark**
- Sequential block with async reset — **1 mark**
- Next-state logic — **2 marks**
- Moore output block — **2 marks**
- One-hot justification comment + default case — **2 marks**
- Clean compile — **3 marks** *(partial credit if minor issues)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Moore and Mealy semantics** in a single question — if the question says "Moore", do not let `din` appear in the output assignment. This costs **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `default:` case** — guaranteed **−2 marks** as it implies a latch.
> 3. **Using `=` (blocking) inside `posedge` blocks** — this is a *fatal* Verilog error. Always use **non-blocking `<=`** for sequential logic.
> 4. **Not declaring output as `reg`** when assigned inside `always` — compilation failure = **0 for that block**.
> 5. **State encoding width mismatch** — declaring `S0=2'b00 ... S3=2'b11` but using a 1-bit `reg` for state. Always match the `reg` width to the encoding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **FSM** is a sequential machine with *finite* memory; in Verilog, the memory is modelled as a `reg` updated by a clocked `always` block.
- A **Moore** output is a function of *state only*; a **Mealy** output is a function of *state and input*.
- The **three-block coding style** is the KTU-preferred structure:
  1. Sequential `always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)` — state register.
  2. Combinational `always @(*)` — next-state logic with `case` + `default`.
  3. Combinational `always @(*)` or `assign` — output logic.
- Always use **non-blocking assignment (`<=`)** inside sequential blocks; use **blocking (`=`)** inside combinational blocks.
- **State encoding width** $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ for binary; $n = N$ for one-hot.
- The `default:` case is **mandatory** in every KTU answer — it prevents inferred latches.
- **Asynchronous active-low reset** (`posedge clk or negedge rst_n`) is the most commonly tested reset style in KTU 2024.
- A Mealy machine typically needs **fewer states** than the equivalent Moore machine, but its outputs may **glitch** with input changes.
- One-hot encoding is **faster on FPGAs** because next-state decoding is a simple 4-to-1 mux, but uses more flip-flops.
- For sequence detectors, the *overlapping* version is more common in KTU exams than the non-overlapping one — read the question carefully.
- Always **comment the Moore/Mealy classification** at the top of your module — this is a free 1-mark win.
- Verilog-2001's `always @(*)` is preferred over the older `always @(state or din)` explicit form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
